summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the formula for orbital speed?

Electrons fill orbitals following the Aufbau principle (lowest energy first), Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital), and Hund's rule (fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing). The answer is 'v = √(GM/r)'. The other options — 'v = GM/r', 'v = √(GM·r)', and 'v = 2πr/GM' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is a geostationary orbit?

For uniform circular motion, the object moves at constant speed in a circle. Centripetal acceleration a_c = v²/r points toward the center. The centripetal force provides this acceleration. The answer is 'An orbit with T = 24 hours staying above the same point'. The other options — 'Any orbit around Earth', 'An orbit at the equator surface', and 'Any satellite with zero velocity' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
